content:
First note that for a positive integer \(n\) with digit‐product \(P(n)\), the equation
\[
P(n)\;=\;n^2-15n-27
\]
can only hold for fairly small \(n\).  Indeed, once \(n\ge21\), the right‐hand side exceeds \(99\), while any two‐digit number has digit‐product at most \(9\cdot9=81\), and any larger \(n\) makes the gap even worse.

Hence it suffices to check \(1\le n\le20\).  A quick table:

\[
\begin{array}{c|c|c}
n&\displaystyle P(n)&\displaystyle n^2-15n-27\\\hline
17&1\cdot7=7&289-255-27=7
\end{array}
\]
(all other \(n\) in that range fail to match).

Thus the only solution is \(n=17\), and the sum of all solutions is
\[
\boxed{17}.
\]
